content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


May 1, 2002


Kathryn E. Donovan
Pillsbury Winthrop
400 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Sacramento, CA 95814-4419

Re:	YOUR REQUEST FOR ADVICE
OUR FILE NO. A-02-078

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of State Treasurer Philip Angelides regarding the blind trust provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹


QUESTIONS

1.	May a certified public accountant ("CPA") who is already known and employed by Mr. Angelides with an interest in a blind trust, but who is not related by family or marriage to him, qualify as a "disinterested party" for purposes of regulation 18235?

2.	After the initial funding of the blind trust, may additional assets be placed in the trust in the future?

3.	May Mr. Angelides prescribe in the trust instrument certain categories of assets to which the trustee would be limited in investing the assets of the trust, with the trustee given complete discretion within the parameters of the various categories?

4.	May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides communicate with the trustee from time to time, but at least annually, concerning their general instruction as to the ranges for allocation of trust assets among the categories of investments listed in Question 3?



___________________________
Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 2

5. May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides include in the trust instrument provisions
concerning voluntary resignation of the trustee and replacement of the trustee with
another disinterested party, as specified in regulation 18235?

6. May the trust instrument include a provision regarding the trustee's ability
to engage one or more professional investment advisors, if she deems it prudent to do so,
as permitted by Probate Code section 16052?

7. May the trust instrument include a provision concerning the distribution of
trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides,
including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net income, net capital gains or
losses of the trust, and to provide information concerning the date of disposition of any
original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include that information on his
Statement of Economic Interests?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. Since the CPA would have no beneficial interest in the trust, and she
is not a prohibited family member, as set forth in regulation 18235, a previously-existing
professional relationship with the filer will not disqualify the CPA from being appointed
trustee of a blind trust under regulation 18235.

2. There is nothing in regulation 18235 to preclude the deposit of new assets
into the trust portfolio after the initial funding of the trust. The new assets would simply
be treated the same way the original assets were, i.e. the filer must report them and take
any necessary disqualification actions until notified by the trustee that they have been
disposed of and replaced.

3. No. Regulation 18235 provides that the trustee is to have complete
discretion in managing the trust. If the filer prescribes the categories of assets in which
the trustee may invest, it would infringe on the trustee's discretion, and would begin to
erode the separation between the trustee and the public official that is critical to the
concept of the blind trust as a vehicle for removing obstacles to investments by public
officials.

4. No. For the same reasons as set forth in the answer to Question 3, to allow
the filer to dictate the allocation of investment concentrations among categories of
investments would remove the trustee's discretion, which is contrary to the intent of
regulation 18235.

5. Yes. Provisions for the voluntary resignation of the trustee and the
replacement of the trustee with someone who fulfills the requirements of regulation
18235(b) are permissible.

6. Yes. The engagement of investment advisors would be within the
trustee's discretion in managing the trust, and such a provision would be acceptable.

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 3

7. Yes. The trust instrument must include a provision requiring the trustee to report the date of disposition of any original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include the information on his Statement of Economic Interests. Provisions regarding distribution of trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides regarding the health of the trust are not precluded by regulation 18235, but must be within the parameters set forth in regulation 18235(b)(4).

ANALYSIS

You are requesting formal written advice, pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b), on behalf of State Treasurer Philip Angelides. Mr. Angelides and his wife, Julanne Angelides, are considering establishing a blind trust, as authorized by regulation 18235. Regulation 18235 permits a public official to establish a blind trust and perform his or her official duties without worry about conflicts of interest with respect to the assets in the trust portfolio, as long as the official complies with certain requirements. While considering whether to establish a blind trust, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides have raised the following questions about the requirements of regulation 18235.

Question 1. Appointment of Trustee: May a certified public accountant who is already known and employed by a filer with an interest in a blind trust but who is not related by family or marriage to the filer, qualify as a "disinterested party" for purposes of regulation 18235?

Facts: Regulation 18235 requires the trustee of the blind trust to be a "disinterested party" other than specified family members of the official's family. Mr. and Mrs. Angelides are considering appointing as trustee the certified public accountant (CPA) who currently performs, for compensation, the accounting and bookkeeping services for Mr. and Mrs. Angelides with respect to personal and business matters. The CPA is not related by family or marriage to Mr. or Mrs. Angelides. She would not be a beneficiary of the trust, although she would receive reasonable compensation for her services (Draft Paragraph 6.6).

Analysis: Regulation 18235(b) provides:

"A blind trust must comply with the following conditions:

"(1) the trustee must be a disinterested party other than the filer's spouse, child, parent, grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-in-law, brother-in-law, sister-in-law, aunt, uncle or first cousin or the spouse of any such person."

As you state in your letter, since the Angelides's current CPA would have no beneficial interest in the trust and is not a prohibited family member, as set forth in the regulation, she qualifies as a "disinterested party" for purposes of regulation 18235 and may be appointed as the trustee of the Angelides's blind trust. (Diaz Advice Letter, Nos.

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 4

A-89-425 and A-89-437.) Her previously existing professional relationship with the
Angelideses would not disqualify her from this role.

Question 2. Additions to the Blind Trust Portfolio: After the initial funding of the
blind trust, may additional assets be placed in the trust in the future?

Facts: Initially, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides intend to fund the trust with assets that
are not considered economic interests under the Act. The Act does not regulate the
method of funding the trust where it is not funded by assets considered economic
interests under the Act. You ask whether later acquired assets that are considered
economic interests may be added to the blind trust in order to avoid disclosure and
disqualification obligations under the Act.

Analysis: Regulation 18235 does not prohibit additions to the trust portfolio.
Pursuant to regulation 18235(a):

"Notwithstanding the provisions of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18234(c), a
filer who has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest in a blind trust which
meets the standards set forth in subsection (b) is not required to disclose
the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real property or investments, or
income deriving from any such interests or investments, if those
investments are acquired by the trustee after the trust complies with
subsection (b).

"However, nothing in this section relieves the filer from his or her
obligation (1) to disclose the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real
property or investments, or income deriving from any such interests or
investments, if the interests or investments were originally transferred into
the trust, and (2) to disqualify himself or herself from participating in
decisions which may have a foreseeable and material effect on financial
interests which are reportable under this regulation."

Although this subdivision refers to the original transfer of assets into the portfolio
of the blind trust, it does not limit this initial transfer to any specific period of time.
Rather, it treats as "original" those assets of which the maker has knowledge. As long as
the filer continues to disclose the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real property or
investments that would be reportable under regulation 18235, and to disqualify himself
or herself from participating in decisions in which he or she has a conflict of interest arising
from those assets, the purposes of the regulation would be furthered by allowing
additions to the trust portfolio. Otherwise, the only option for an official wishing to use
the blind trust device on a continual basis would be to establish a new blind trust each
time he or she wished to deposit new assets into the trust portfolio over time. This is

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 5

impractical and serves no purpose that cannot be served just as well by simply allowing
additions to be made to existing blind trust portfolios.²

Question 3. Assets Included in the Blind Trust Portfolio: May Mr. Angelides
prescribe in the trust instrument certain categories of assets to which the trustee would be
limited in investing the assets of the trust, with the trustee given complete discretion
within the parameters of the various categories?

Facts: As a general rule, trustees are bound by the "prudent investor" standard,
as set forth in the California Uniform Investor Act, Probate Code sections 16045-16054,
which will apply to this trust (Draft Paragraph 6.10). In addition, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides
wish to include a provision that investment would be permitted in the following five
broad categories of assets, with the trustee given complete discretion within these
parameters:

• Domestic public equities (e.g., stock in U.S. publicly-held companies)
• International public equities (e.g., stock in foreign publicly-held companies)
• Private fixed income instruments (e.g., corporate bonds)
• Domestic public fixed income instruments (e.g., government bonds)
• Cash and cash equivalents (e.g., money market accounts)

Analysis: As you note in your letter, there are no Commission staff advice letters
or Commission opinions to provide guidance on this issue. However, regulation 18235
was created as an exception to the general rules related to the reporting of trust assets, to
afford public officials a vehicle by which to take advantage of various investment
opportunities without running foul of the Act's conflict of interest provisions. It is a
fundamental canon of statutory construction that exceptions are to be construed strictly
and narrowly. Ticket Track California, Inc. v. Department of Motor Vehicles, 2002 WL
725211, 3 (Cal. App. 3d Dist., April 25, 2002).

With respect to communications between the filer and the trustee, regulation
18235(b) provides:

"A blind trust must comply with the following conditions:

¶ . . . ¶

"(2) the trustee must be given complete discretion to manage the trust
including, but not limited to, the power to dispose of and acquire trust
assets without consulting or notifying the filer;

"(3) the trustee must be required to notify the filer of the date of
disposition and value at disposition of any original investments or interests

___________________________
² Please note that this letter only pertains to assets liquidated through the trust. If the official
should sell an asset outside the blind trust, other disqualifying economic interests may be created, such as
source of income.

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 6

in real property so that information can be reported on the filer's next
Statement of Economic Interests;

"(4) the trustee must be prohibited from disclosing to the filer any
information concerning the replacement assets except for information
required under this subsection or the minimum tax information which lists
only the totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual item of income . . .."

Allowing the kinds of "broad categories of investments" that you seek would
infringe on the autonomy of the trustee in his or her ability to manage the blind trust. The
regulation provides that "the trustee must be given complete discretion to manage the
trust." (Reg. 18235(b)(2).) As the regulation is to be construed narrowly, we believe that
to allow the filer to dictate "broad categories of investments" to which the trustee would
be limited would compromise the trustee's discretion in managing the trust.

This is consistent with the treatment of blind trust arrangements under the federal
regulation related to communications between filer and trustee. In all material respects,
the federal and California visions of the blind trust vehicle appear to be quite parallel.³
The federal regulation, with respect to communications between parties with beneficial
interests and trustees, states:

"For purposes of Federal ethics laws, the most important feature of those
qualified trusts that are recognized under the Act is the separation which
those trusts foster between parties with beneficial interests in the trust and
entities which manage trust assets and administer the trust instrument.
Once a qualified trust has been certified, the beneficiaries and their
representatives are expressly prohibited from commenting directly to the
trustee about matters relating to asset management and trust holdings, or to
trust administration and activities. Likewise, the trustee must make
investment decisions for the trust without consulting, or being controlled
by, interested parties, and the trustee is prohibited from informing
interested parties directly about trust activities, except to the limited extent
required under the Act. . . ." (5 C.F.R. § 2634.401(a)(3))⁴

³ Although the federal statute and regulation and the California regulation governing blind trusts
differ in several respects, the function and the purpose to be served by the trusts is the same under both
schemes. The most notable differences are that, under the federal scheme, (1) the trust instrument must
conform to a particular format and must be approved by the Government Ethics Office; (2) any
communications between the beneficiaries and trustee must be in writing and must first be approved by the
Government Ethics Office; and (3) the trustee may not be an individual, but must be a financial institution.
(5 C.F.R. § 2634.401(a).)
⁴ The federal Office of Government Ethics has issued several advice letters construing the federal
regulation. In a letter to a federal employee dated February 28, 1996, No. 96 x 4, the Office of Government
Ethics addressed precisely the question you pose. The Ethics Office stated that the individual judgments of
the parties to a blind trust arrangement could not be substituted for the clear dictates of the statute.

"There are particular provisions of the statute that are relevant to the issues you raise.
First, paragraph (3)(C)(1) of section 102(f) [of 5 U.S.C. App. 4] directs that 'the trustee in

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 7

We note that in our conversation on April 9, 2002, you stated that the primary
purpose for including the categories is actually to preclude the trustee from investing the
assets of the blind trust in real estate, because of Mr. Angelides's long-time involvement
in that industry. You expressed Mr. Angelides's concern that, because of his long-time
involvement in the real estate business and his continuing contact with people currently
involved in the business, it would be virtually impossible for him to remain ignorant of
any real estate investments made on his behalf by the trustee. Thus, the concern is that
the trust would not be truly blind. However, regulation 18235 provides for just such an
occurrence:

"(b) A blind trust must comply with the following conditions:

¶ . . . ¶

"(5) if the trust is revoked while the filer is a public official, or if the filer
learns of any replacement assets of the trust, the filer must file an
amendment to the most recent Statement of Economic Interests disclosing
the date of revocation and the previously unreported pro rata share of the
trust's interests in real property or investments or income deriving from
such interests in real property or investments and disqualify himself or
herself, as necessary. For purposes of this regulation, any replacement
assets of which the filer learns shall thereafter be treated as though they
were original assets of the trust." (Regulation 18235.)

Therefore, it is not necessary to make a special provision for such an event in the
trust instrument. Mr. Angelides can simply follow the procedures as outlined in the
regulation, in the event he learns the specifics of a replacement asset.

Question 4. Communications to Trustee Concerning Asset Allocation Ranges: May
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides communicate with the trustee from time to time, but at least
annually, concerning their general instructions as to the ranges for allocation of trust
assets among the categories of investments listed in Question 3?

Facts: Mr. and Mrs. Angelides propose to communicate to the trustee from time
to time, but at least annually, concerning their general instructions as to the ranges for
allocation of trust assets among the broad categories of investments listed in Question 3,

the exercise of his authority and discretion to manage and control the assets of the trust
shall not consult or notify any interested party.' The import of this provision, which by
extension applies to the investment manager of your trust as well, is that there cannot be a
discussion or exchange of views about market conditions and strategy between you and
the fiduciaries. Once the fiduciaries are selected, they are to function without you
'consultation' with you."

In another letter issued by the Office of Government Ethics to the general counsel of an agency
dated September 8, 1986, No. 86 x 12, the Ethics Office stated, "Our position is that the statutory scheme
for blind trusts does not permit constraints on the investment powers of the trustee of a qualified trust."

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 8

above. For example, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides might state that public domestic equities
should constitute between 25 and 50 percent of the total value of the trust portfolio.

Analysis: As outlined above in the answer to Question 3, regulation 18235(b)(2)
provides that trustee is to have complete discretion to manage the trust without consulting
or notifying the filer. Having Mr. and Mrs. Angelides in control of the allocation of the
assets among investment vehicles would infringe upon the trustee's discretion to manage
the trust and is not permitted under regulation 18235.

Question 5. Voluntary Resignation of Trustee: May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides include
in the trust instrument provisions concerning voluntary resignation of the trustee and
replacement of the trustee with another disinterested party, as specified in regulation
18235?

Facts: In addition to a standard trust provision governing the voluntary
resignation of the trustee, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides would include a provision concerning
replacement of the trustee (Draft Paragraph 6.2). It appears that a provision concerning
replacement of the trustee with another disinterested trustee, as specified in regulation
18235, would be permitted.

Analysis: There is nothing in regulation 18235 that would preclude these
provisions, and such provisions would not interfere in any way with the purpose of the
blind trust. Therefore, they are acceptable under regulation 18235.

Question 6. Engagement of Investment Advisors: May the trust instrument include a
provision regarding the trustee's ability to engage one or more professional investment
advisors, if she deems it prudent to do so, as permitted by Probate Code section 16052?

Facts: Another example is the trustee's ability to engage one or more
professional investment advisors. The trust also would include a provision that would
permit the trustee to engage investment advisors if she deems it prudent to do so, as
permitted by Probate Code section 16052 (Draft Paragraph 6.12). Again, such a
provision appears to be permitted by regulation 18235.

Analysis: Again, there is nothing in regulation 18235 that would preclude a
provision allowing for the engagement of investment advisors if, in the independent
judgment of the trustee, it seemed prudent to do so. Indeed, such a provision would seem
to further the purposes of securing the financial security of the public official, while
maintaining the confidentiality of the investment portfolio. Therefore, we agree that such
a provision is acceptable under regulation 18235.

Question 7. May the trust instrument include a provision concerning the distribution of
trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides,
including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net income, net capital gains or
losses of the trust, and to provide information concerning the date of disposition of any
original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include that information on his
Statement of Economic Interests?

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 9

Facts: Finally, the trust would include provisions concerning the distribution
of trust income and principal (Draft Paragraph 4.2), and the trustee's duty to account to
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides, including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net
income, net capital gains or losses of the trust, and to provide information concerning the
date of disposition of any original reportable trust assets so that Mr. Angelides can
include that information on his Statement of Economic Interests (Draft Paragraphs 6.15 -
6.17).

Analysis: Pursuant to regulation 18235(b)(3):

"[T]he trustee must be required to notify the filer of the date of disposition
and value at disposition of any original investments or interests in real
property so that information can be reported on the filer's next Statement
of Economic Interests."

Therefore, the trust instrument, necessarily, must provide for the reporting of this
information. Likewise, logic dictates the conclusion that the trust instrument must
provide for the distribution of trust income and principal. However, regulation
18235(b)(4) provides:

"[T]he trustee must be prohibited from disclosing to the filer any
information concerning the replacement assets except for information
required under this subsection or the minimum tax information which lists
only the totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual item of income."

As discussed above, exceptions are to be construed narrowly. Both regulations
18235(b)(3) and 18235(b)(4), referring to notification to the filer of information for
reporting on the filer's Statement of Economic Interests and tax return, respectively, seem
to imply an annual notification, as the purposes for which the notifications take place are
annual filings. Regulation 18235(b)(4) is very specific as to the information related to
the replacement assets that the trustee may disclose, limiting it to only "information
required under [subsection (b)] or the minimum tax information which lists only the
totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the source of any individual
item of income."

Therefore, with respect to the provision regarding the trustees' duty to account to
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides on a quarterly basis and to disclose net income, net capital gains
or losses of the trust, this provision is allowable to the extent the information provided is
be limited to the parameters set forth in regulation 18235(b)(4).

Please be advised that if you wish to seek reconsideration of these issues from the
Commission, that avenue is available to you through the opinion process set forth in
regulation 18320.

File No. A-02-078
Page No. 10

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Holly B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.facts:
Regulation 18235 requires the trustee of the blind trust to be a
"disinterested party" other than specified family members of the official's family.
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides are considering appointing as trustee the certified public
accountant (CPA) who currently performs, for compensation, the accounting and
bookkeeping services for Mr. and Mrs. Angelides with respect to personal and business
matters. The CPA is not related by family or marriage to Mr. or Mrs. Angelides. She
would not be a beneficiary of the trust, although she would receive reasonable
compensation for her services (Draft Paragraph 6.6).
Analysis: Regulation I 8235(b) provides:
"A blind trust must comply with the following conditions:
"(1) the trustee must be a disinterested party other than the filer's spouse,
child, parent, grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-inJaw,
brother-in-law, sister-in-law, aunt, uncle or first cousin or the spouse of
any such person."
As you state in your letter, since the Angelides's current CPA would have no
beneficial interest in the trust and is not a prohibited family member, as set forth in the
regulation, she qualifies as a "disinterested party" for purposes ofregulation 18235 and
may be appointed as the trustee of the Angelides's blind trust. (Diaz Advice Letteq Nos.
A-89-425 and A-89-437.) Her previously existing professional relationship with the
Angelideses would not disqualify her from this role.

Question 2. Additions to the Blind Trust Portfolio: After the initial funding of the
blind trust, may additional assets be placed in the trust in the future?
Facts: Initially, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides intend to fund the trust with assets that
are not considered economic interests under the Act. The Act does not regulate the
method of funding the trust where it is not funded by assets considered economic
interests under the Act. You ask whether later acquired assets that are considered
economic interests may be added to the blind trust in order to avoid disclosure and
disqualification obligations under the Act.
Analysis: Regulation 18235 does not prohibit additions to the trust portfolio
Pursuant to regulation 18235(a):
"Notwithstanding the provisions of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18234(c), a
filer who has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest in a blind trust which
meets the standards set forth in subsection (b) is not required to disclose
the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real property or investments, or
income deriving from any such interests or investments, if those
investments are acquired by the trustee after the trust complies with
subsection (b).
"However, nothing in this section relieves the filer from his or her
obligation (1) to disclose the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real
property or investments, or income deriving from any such interests or
investments, if the interests or investments were originally transferred into
the trust, and (2) to disqualify himself or herself from participating in
decisions which may have a foreseeable and material effect on financial
interests which are reportable under this regulation."
Although this subdivision refers to the original transfer of assets into the portfolio
of the blind trust, it does not limit this initial transfer to any specific period of time.
Rather, it treats as "original" those assets of which the maker has knowledge. As long as
the filer continues to disclose the pro rata share of the trust's interests in real property or
investments that would be reportable under regulation 18235, and to disqualify himself or
herself from participating in decisions in which he or she has a conflict of interest arising
from those assets, the purposes of the regulation would be furthered by allowing
additions to the trust portfolio. Otherwise, the only option for an official wishing to use
the blind trust device on a continual basis would be to establish a new blind trust each
time he or she wished to deposit new assets into the trust portfolio over time. This is
impraclical and serves no purpose that cannot be served just as well by simply allowing
additions to be made to existing blind trust portfolios.2
Question 3. Assets Included in the Blind Trust Portfolio: May IMr. Angelides
prescribe in the trust instrument certain categories of assets to which the trustee would be
limited in investing the assets of the trus! with the trustee given complete discretion
within the parameters of the various categories?
Facts: As a general rule, trustees are bound by the "prudent investor" standard,
as set forth in the California Uniform Investor Act, Probate Code sections 16045-16054,
which will apply to this trust (Draft Paragraph 6.10). In addition, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides
wish to include a provision that investment would be permitted in the following five
broad categories of assets, with the trustee given complete discretion within these
parameters:
. Domestic public equities (e.g., stock in U. S. publicly-held companies)
'International public equities (e.g., stock in foreign publicly-held companies)
. Private fixed income instruments (e.g., corporate bonds)
. Domestic public fixed income instruments (e.g., government bonds)
. Cash and cash equivalents (e.g., money market accounts)
Analysis: As you note in your letter, there are no Commission staffadvice letters
or Commission opinions to provide guidance on this issue. However, regulation 18235

afford public officials a vehicle by which to take advantage ofvarious investment
opportunities without running afoul of the Act's conflict of interest provisions. It is a
fundamental canon of statutory construction that exceptions are to be construed strictly
and narrowly. Ticket Track California, Inc. v. Department of Motor Vehicles, 2OO2WL
725211,3 (Cal. App. 3d Dist., April25,2002).
With respect to communications between the filer and the trustee, regulation
18235(b) provides:
"A blind trust must comply with the following conditions
1l 1T
"(2) the trustee must be given complete discretion to manage the trust
including, but not limited to, the power to dispose of and acquire trust
assets without consulting or notifying the filer;
"(3) the trustee must be required to notifu the filer of the date of
disposition and value at disposition of any original investments or interests
2 Please note that this letter only pertains to assets liquidated through the trust. If the ofhcial
should se1l an asset outside the blind trust, other disqualiSing economic interests may be created, such as
source of income.
in real property so that information can be reported on the filer's next
Statement of Economic Interests;
"(4) the trustee must be prohibited from disclosing to the filer any
information concerning the replacement assets except for information
required under this subsection or the minimum tax information which lists
only the totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual item of income . . ."
Allowing the kinds of "broad categories of investments" that you seek would
infringe on the autonomy of the trustee in his or her ability to manage the blind trust. The
regulation provides that"the trustee must be given complete discretion to manage the
trust." (Reg. 18235(b)(2).) As the regulation is to be construed narrowly, we believe that
to allow the filer to dictate "broad categories of investments" to which the trustee would
be limited would compromise the trustee's discretion in managing the trust.
This is consistent with the treatment of blind trust arrangements under the federal
regulation related to communications between filer and trustee. In all material respects,
the federal and California visions of the blind trust vehicle appear to be quite parallel.3
The federal regulation, with respect to communications between parties with beneficial
interests and trustees, states:
"For purposes of Federal ethics laws, the most important feature of those
qualified trusts that are recognized under the Act is the separation which
those trusts foster between parties with beneficial interests in the trust and
entities which marLage trust assets and administer the trust instrument.
Once a qualified trust has been certified, the beneficiaries and their
representatives are expressly prohibited from comrnenting directly to the
trustee about matters relating to asset management and trust holdings, or to
trust administration and activities. Likewise, the trustee must make
investment decisions for the trust without consulting, or being controlled
by, interested parties, and the trustee is prohibited from informing
interested parties directly about trust activities, except to the limited extent
required under the Act. . . ." (5 C.F.R. S 2634.401(aX:) f
3 Although the federal statute and regulation and the Califomia regulation governing blind trusts
differ in several respects, the function and the purpose to be served by the trusts is the same rmderboth
schemes. The most notable differences are that, under the federal scheme, (1) the trust instrument must
conform to a particular format and must be approved by the Government Ethics Offrce; (2) Nry
communications between the beneficiaries and trustee must be in miting and must first be approved by the
Government Ethics Office; and (3) the trustee may not be an individual, but mustbe a financial institution.
(5 C.F.R. $ 2634.401(a).)
a The federal Offrce of Government Ethics has issued several advice letters construing the federal
regulation. In a letter to a federal employee dated Febnrary 28, T996, No. 96 x 4, the Office of Government
Ethics addressed precisely the question you pose. The Ethics Office stated that the individual judgments of
the parties to a blind trust arrangement could not be substituted for the clear dictates ofthe statute.
"There are particular provisions ofthe statute tlat are relevant to the issues you raise.
First, paragraph (3XC)(I) of section 102(f [of 5 U.S.C. App 4] directs that 'rhe rrustee in
We note that rn our conversation on April 9,2002, you stated that the primary
purpose for including the categories is actually to preclude the trustee from investing the
assets of the blind trust in real estate, because of Mr. Angelides's long-time involvement
in that industry. You expressed Mr. Angelides's concern that, because of his long-time
involvement in the real estate business and his continuing contact with people currently
involved in the business, it would be virtually impossible for him to remain ignorant of
any real estate investments made on his behalf by the trustee. Thus, the concern is that
the trust would not be truly blind. However, regulation 18235 provides for just such an
occurrence:
"(b) A blind trust must comply with the following conditions
11 1I
"(5) if the trust is revoked while the filer is a public official, or if the filer
learns of any replacement assets of the trust, the filer must file an
amendment to the most recent Statement of Economic Interests disclosing
the date ofrevocation and the previously unreported pro rata share ofthe
trust's interests in real property or investments or income deriving from
such interests in real property or investments and disqualify himself or
herself as necessary. For purposes of this regulation, any replacement
assets of which the filer learns shall thereafter be treated as though they
were original assets of the trust." (Regulation 18235.)
Therefore, it is not necessary to make a special provision for such an event in the
trust instrument. Mr. Angelides can simply follow the procedures as outlined in the
regulation, in the event he learns the specifics of a replacement asset.
Question 4. Communications to Trustee Concerning Asset Allocation Ranges: May
Mr. and Mrs Angelides communicate with the trustee from time to time, but at least
annually, concerning their general instructions as to the ranges for allocation of trust
assets among the categories of investments listed in Question 3?
Facts: Mr. and Mrs. Angelides propose to communicate to the trustee from time
to time, but at least annually, concerning their general instructions as to the ranges for
allocation of trust assets among the broad categories of investments listed in Question 3,
the exercise of his authority and discretion to manage and control the assets ofthe trust
shall not consult or notiff any interested party.' The import of this provision, which by
extension applies to the investment manager of your trust as well, is that there cannot be a
discussion or exchange of views about market conditions and strategy between you and
the fiduciaries. Once the fiduciaries are selected, they are to firnction without
' consultation' with you. "
In another letter issued by the Office of Government Ethics to the general counsel of an agency
dated September 8, 1986, No. 86 x 12, the Ethics Office stated, "Our position is that the statutory scheme
for blind trusts does not permit constraints on the investment powers of the trustee of a qualified trust."
above. For example, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides might state that public domestic equities
should constitute between 25 and 50 percent ofthe total value of the trust portfolio.
Analysis: As outlined above in the answer to Question 3, regulation 18235(b)(Z)
provides that trustee is to have complete discretion to manage the trust without consulting
or notifying the filer. Having Mr. and Mrs. Angelides in control of the allocation of the
assets among investment vehicles would infringe upon the trustee's discretion to manage
the trust and is not permitted under regulation 18235.
Question 5. Voluntary Resignation of Trustee: May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides include
in the trust instrument provisions concerning voluntary resignation of the trustee and
replacement of the trustee with another disinterested party, as specified in regulation
18235?
Facts: In addition to a standard trust provision governing the voluntary
resignation of the trustee, Mr. and Mrs. Angelides would include a provision concerning
replacement of the trustee (Draft Paragraph 6.2). It appears that aprovision concerning
replacement of the trustee with another disinterested trustee, as specified in regulation
18235, would be permitted.
Analysis: There is nothing in regulation 18235 that would preclude these
provisions, and such provisions would not interfere in any way with the purpose of the
blind trust. Therefore, they are acceptable under regulation 18235.
Question 6. Engagement of Investment Advisors: May the trust instrument include a
provision regarding the trustee's ability to engage one or more professional investment
advisors, if she deems it prudent to do so, as permitted by Probate Code section 16052?
Facts: Another example is the trustee's ability to engage one or more
professional investment advisors. The trust also would include a provision that would
permit the trustee to engage investment advisors if she deems it prudent to do so, as
permitted by Probate Code section 16052 (Draft Paragraph 6.12). Again, such a
provision appears to be permitted by regulation 15235.
Analysis: Again, there is nothing in regulation 18235 that would preclude a
provision allowing for the engagement of investment advisors if in the independent
judgment of the trustee, it seemed prudent to do so. Indeed, such a provision would seem
to further the purposes of securing the financial security of the public official, while
maintaining the confidentiality of the investment portfolio. Therefore, we agree that such
a provision is acceptable under regulation 18235.
Question 7.May the trust instrument include a provision concerning the distribution of
trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides,
including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net income, net capital gains or
losses of the trus! and to provide information concerning the date of disposition of any
original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include that information on his
Statement of Economic Interests?
f,'acts: Finally, the trust would include provisions concerning the distribution
of trust income and principal @raft Paragraph 4.2), andthe trustee's duty to account to
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides, including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net
income, net capttal gains or losses of the trust, and to provide information concerning the
date of disposition of any original reportable trust assets so that Mr. Angelides can
include that information on his Statement of Economic Interests (Draft Paragraphs 6.15 -
6.17),
Analysis: Pursuant to regulation I 8235(b)(3)
"[T]he trustee must be required to notify the filer of the date of disposition
and value at disposition of any original investments or interests in real
property so that information can be reported on the filer's next Statement
of Economic Interests. "
Therefore, the trust instrument, necessarily, must provide for the reporting of this
information. Likewise, logic dictates the conclusion that the trust instrument must
provide for the distribution of trust income and principal. However, regulation
18235(b)(4) provides:
"[T]he trustee must be prohibited from disclosing to the filer any
information concerning the replacement assets except for information
required under this subsection or the minimum ta>r information which lists
only the totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual item of income."
As discussed above, exceptions are to be construed narrowly. Both regulations
18235(bX3) and 18235(bX4), refering to notification to the filer of information for
reporting on the filer's Statement of Economic Interests and tax return, respectively, seem
to imply an annual notification, as the purposes for which the notificationslake place are
annual filings. Regulation 18235(b)(4) is very specific as to the information relited to
the replacement assets that the trustee may disclose, limiting it to only "information
required under [subsection (b)] or the minimum tax information which lists only the
totals of taxable items from the trust and does not describe the source of any individual
item of income."
Therefore, with respect to the provision regarding the trustees' duty to account to
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides on a quarterly basis and to disclose net income, net capital gains
or losses of the trust, this provision is allowable to the extent the information provided is
be limited to the parameters set forth in regulation 18235(b)(a).
Please be advised that if you wish to seek reconsideration of these issues from the
Commission, that avenue is available to you through the opinion process set forth in
regulation 18320.


Page No. l0
sections.analysis:
You are requesting formal written advice, pursuant to Government Code section
83 1 14(b), on behalf of State Treasurer Philip Angelides. Mr. Angelides and his wife,
Julanne Angelides, are considering establishing a blind trust, as authorized by regulation
18235. Regulation 18235 permits a public official to establish a blind trust and perform
his or her official duties without worry about conflicts of interest with respect to the
assets in the trust portfolio, as long as the official complies with certain requirements,
While considering whether to establish a blind trus! Mr. and Mrs. Angelides have raised
the following questions about the requirements of regulation 1g235.
Question 1. Appointment of Trustee: May a certified public accountant who is already
known and employed by a fiIer with an interest in a blind trus! but who is not related by
family or marriage to the filer, qualify as a "disinterested party" for purposes of
regulation 18235?

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: L 
May a certified public accountant ("cpA") who is already known and
employed by Mr. Angelides with an interest in a blind trust, but who is not related by
family or marriage to him, qualify as a "disinterested party" for purposes of regulation
18235?
2
After the initial funding of the blind trust, may additional assets be placed
in the trust in the future?
3. 
May Mr. Angelides prescribe in the trust instrument certain categories of
assets to which the trustee would be limited in investing the assets of the trust, with the
trustee given complete discretion within the parameters of the various categories?
4. 
May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides communicate with the trustee from time to
time, but at least annually, concerning their general instruction as to the ranges for
allocation of trust assets among the categories of investments listed in Question 3?
€
5. 
May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides include in the trust instrument provisions
concerning voluntary resignation of the trustee and replacement of the trustee with
another disinterested party, as specified in regulation 18235?
6. 
May the trust instrument include a provision regarding the trustee's ability
to engage one or more professional investment advisors, if she deems it prudent to do so,
as permitted by Probate Code section 16052?
7. 
May the trust instrument include a provision concerning the distribution of
trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides,
including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net income, net capitalgains or
losses of the trusf and to provide information concerning the date of disposition of any
original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include that information on his
Statement of Economic Interests?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Yes. Since the CPA would have no beneficial interest in the trust, and she
is not a prohibited family member, as set forth in regulation 18235, a previously-existing
professional relationship with the filer will not disqualify the CPA from being appointed
trustee of a blind trust under regulation 18235.
2. 
There is nothing in regulation L8235 to preclude the deposit of new assets
into the trust portfolio after the initial funding of the trust. The new assets would simply
be treated the same way the original assets were, i.e. the filer must report them and take
any necessary disqualification actions until notified by the trustee that they have been
disposed of and replaced.
3. 
No. Regulation 18235 provides that the trustee is to have complete
discretion in managing the trust. If the filer prescribes the categories of assets in which
the trustee may invest, it would infringe on the trustee's discretion, and would begin to
erode the separation between the trustee and the public official that is critical to the
concept of the blind trust as a vehicle for removing obstacles to investments by public
officials.
4. 
No. For the same reasons as set forth in the answer to Question 3, to allow
the filer to dictate the allocation of investment concentrations among categories of
investments would remove the trustee's discretion, which is contrary to the intent of
regulation 18235
5. 
Yes. Provisions for the voluntary resignation of the trustee and the
replacement of the trustee with someone who fulfills the requirements of regulation
1823 5 (b) are permissible.
6. 
Yes. The engagement of investment advisors would be within the
trustee's discretion in managing the trust, and such a provision would be acceptable
7. 
Yes. The trust instrument must include a provision requiring the trustee to
report the date of disposition of any original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides
can include the information on his Statement of Economic Interests. Provisions
regarding distribution of trust income and princip al, and the trustee's duty to account to
Mr. and Mrs. Angelides regarding the health of the trust are not precluded by regulation
18235, but must be within the parameters set forth in regulation lgz35(b)(4).

Donovan Pillsbury Winthrop 400 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700 Sacramento, CA 95814-4419 Re: YOUR REQUEST FOR ADVICE OUR FILE NO. A-02-078 Dear Ms. Donovan: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of State Treasurer Philip Angelides regarding the blind trust provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. May a certified public accountant ("CPA") who is already known and employed by Mr. Angelides with an interest in a blind trust, but who is not related by family or marriage to him, qualify as a "disinterested party" for purposes of regulation 18235? 2. After the initial funding of the blind trust, may additional assets be placed in the trust in the future? 3. May Mr. Angelides prescribe in the trust instrument certain categories of assets to which the trustee would be limited in investing the assets of the trust, with the trustee given complete discretion within the parameters of the various categories? 4. May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides communicate with the trustee from time to time, but at least annually, concerning their general instruction as to the ranges for allocation of trust assets among the categories of investments listed in Question 3? ___________________________ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-02-078 Page No. 2 5. May Mr. and Mrs. Angelides include in the trust instrument provisions concerning voluntary resignation of the trustee and replacement of the trustee with another disinterested party, as specified in regulation 18235? 6. May the trust instrument include a provision regarding the trustee's ability to engage one or more professional investment advisors, if she deems it prudent to do so, as permitted by Probate Code section 16052? 7. May the trust instrument include a provision concerning the distribution of trust income and principal, and the trustee's duty to account to Mr. and Mrs. Angelides, including the duty to, on a quarterly basis, disclose the net income, net capital gains or losses of the trust, and to provide information concerning the date of disposition of any original reportable trust assets, so that Mr. Angelides can include that information on his Statement of Economic Interests? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. Since the CPA would have no beneficial interest in the trust, and she is not a prohibited family member, as set forth in regulation 18235, a previously-existing professional relationship with the filer will not disqualify the CPA from being appointed trustee of a blind trust under regulation 18235. 2. There is nothing in regulation 18235 to preclude the deposit of new assets into the trust portfolio after the initial funding of the trust. The new assets would simply be treated the same way the original assets were, i.e. the filer must report them and take any necessary disqualification actions until notified by the trustee
